You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — elements-of-the-jazz-language

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc

Jerry Coker's *Elements of the Jazz Language for the Developing Improvisor* (1991) presents jazz improvisation as a finite, learnable language built from a small set of named devices. The thesis, established in Chapter 1, is that the players studied share roughly 18 common devices that account for nearly all the language they speak, and that a student can acquire fluency by mastering each named element in sequence and then learning to combine them. The book's overall structure moves from the most general devices (change-running, digital and scalar patterns) through harmonic-melodic refinements (7-3 resolution, 3-b9, the bebop scale and bebop lick, harmonic generalization, enclosure, sequence, CESH, quotes) to specific stock licks (Cry Me A River, Gone But Not Forgotten) and chromatic-substitution material, closing with analytical considerations (bar-line shifts, side-slipping, errors) and a methodological appendix applying every element to real tune progressions.

## Core Pedagogical Method

The book's master practice methodology, made explicit in Chapter 17 (Appendix B), is a four-stage assimilation: (1) mental understanding through definitions and illustrations, (2) hearing the element used by masters of the craft, (3) hands-on practice in all 12 keys, tempos and registers, (4) ear-to-harmony association built through play-along exercise tracks, and finally (5) application to real tune progressions. Each device gets the same three-stage local progression — practice alone, then with an exercise track that matches the chord-type, then with a play-along tune whose progression offers many opportunities to deploy the device. Practice spans four transposition sequences (chromatic up/down, cycle of fifths, minor thirds, whole steps) and five chord-extension levels (triad through 13th). The book is uncompromising that intellectual understanding is necessary but insufficient: improvisation is a performer's art and the hands must build habit before an element appears in the heat of battle.

## Universalization and the Digital Method

A unifying technique across the book is **digital analysis** (Chapter 2) — naming intervals by scale degree (1-3-5-1) rather than note name (C-E-G-C) so that any exercise becomes instantly transposable to all 12 keys. Chapter 3 elevates this into Baker's named **digital patterns** (4-8 note cells like 1-2-3-5, 1-2-3-4-5-3-2-1) and **scalar patterns** (longer, perpetual-motion runs through a single scale), with generative rules for retrograde, fifth-rooting, and chord-quality alteration. A Coltrane "Giant Steps" analysis shows digital patterns at 35-40% density across the solo, evidence that pattern study is not optional and that even Coltrane practiced patterns apart from the tunes they later served.

## Voice-Leading and Compression Devices

Chapters 4-8 build a vocabulary of dense melodic devices. The **7-3 resolution** (Chapter 4) compresses change-running by using two notes to imply two chords (b7 of one chord → 3 of the next, most often over II-7 to V7). The **3-b9** (Chapters 5-6) targets the flatted 9th of a V7 from its 3rd, bidirectionally and either by leap or scalar fill, and chains with 7-3 via a shared pivot note. The **bebop scale** (Chapters 6-7) adds one specific chromatic note to the major, dorian, or mixolydian scale to land chord tones on downbeats in 4/4; brief 3-4 note fragments containing the chromatic note already imply bebop-scale use. The **bebop lick** (Chapter 8) — Baker's term again — is a short phrase derived from the bebop scale, easily acquired and routinely woven into longer linear material, and extensible to tri-tone substitution contexts.

## Generalization, Enclosure, Sequence, CESH

Chapter 9 develops **harmonic generalization** — one scale across multiple chords — ordered from safest to most advanced: tonic major over II-V-I, pentatonic over a documented ten chords without collision, blues scale over progressions in the Parker tradition, harmonic minor over minor II-V-I (collision-tone but justified by progression brevity, aggregate minor identity, and players' instinctive avoidance of collisions), and the diminished scale over the minor II-V. Chapter 10 introduces **enclosure** — the upper leading tone, lower leading tone, object tone formula — and warns that real enclosures are routinely embellished and that elements combine: a single two-measure phrase can contain six elements interlocked. Chapters 10-11 elevate **sequence** above all other devices as the syntactic level of the language: where digital patterns and 7-3 resolutions are "letters and words," sequences are "complete thoughts" and "chains of thought," and they may be melodic, rhythmic, harmonic, or interval-based, transparent or disguised. Chapters 11-13 cover **CESH** — Contrapuntal Elaboration of Static Harmony — with its four varieties (minor/major × root/fifth in motion), the minor/root variant being most common, deployable over minor chords of long duration but also over II-7, V7, and the combined II-V.

## Quotes, Stock Licks, and Chromatic Substitutions

Chapters 13-14 define **quotes** as phrases from a known tune deployed when the harmonic setting matches the source; quotes are the hardest element because the prerequisite is broad tune repertoire spanning classical (Bizet, Chopin), commercial standards, and recorded jazz solos. Quote competence requires three traits: farcical-yet-poetic humor, quick-witted real-time recognition, and harmonic-setting awareness. Chapters 12 and 15 supply two named stock licks — **Cry Me A River** and **Gone But Not Forgotten** — each capable of fitting five chord-types unaltered (-7, 7, major, altered dominant, #5 major) by starting on different scale degrees. Chapter 15 then defines the remaining chromatic-substitution vocabulary: **linear chromaticism** as metric-fill, **tri-tone substitution** of dominant 7 chords, the **altered dominant** (+5, b9, +9, +11) as structurally near-identical to the tri-tone substitute, the **back door progression** (IV-7 to bVII7 replacing V7), and **#II°7** as a V7 substitute — all of which sound consonant because the listener's ear hears the familiar substitute precedent rather than its conflict with the original V7.

## Analytical Considerations and Application

Chapter 16 closes the named-element catalogue with analytical considerations. **Bar-line shifts** are legitimate phenomena (Cannonball Adderley's intentional half-step bridge displacement on "So What" is the canonical deliberate example); the analyst's rule is to check chords before and after a suspected error. **Side-slipping** and **outside playing** are defined as the same deliberate tension device, with five "wrong" notes available per chord (12 chromatic minus 7 scale tones), used in convincing groups or smooth single-note placements. **Errors** are defined residually — what cannot be reconciled to any named chromatic device — with diagnostic cues including inexplicable space after the wrong note. The chapter offers the book's central methodological caveat: the catalogued elements are connective tissue, and the most original portions of a master's solo are often what remains after every element is tagged.

## Closing Methodology

Appendix A (Chapter 16) provides chord-progression tables for all play-along tracks; Appendix B (Chapter 17) applies elements to three real tune progressions ("Homesick Hoosier," "Urbane Blues," a third tune in Chapter 18's tables) in two-version form — first a scale-recommendation chart for harmonic internalization, then an annotated chart with element placements. The annotation system uses a master legend (7-3, BBL, HG, CMAR, GBNF, TT/AD, BD, #II°) with parenthetical scale or key cues. Coker explicitly identifies which elements are location-independent and therefore absent from annotations (sequences, change-running, digital and scalar patterns, bebop scale, enclosures, quotes, linear chromaticism, bar-line shifts, side-slipping) and insists their absence does not diminish their importance. The book's underlying claim, repeated throughout, is that style is the player's individual emphasis, omission, and combination of these named elements, and that the path to fluency is patient assimilation across the four-stage method until elements arise spontaneously in performance.

# Chapter 1 — CHANGE RUNNING

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes the foundational premise of the method: that jazz language can be learned and taught through a finite, prescriptive set of core devices that every student should master in order to communicate fluently with other jazz musicians. The book identifies eighteen devices as the common vocabulary shared across all studied jazz players, with these elements accounting for nearly all the language used in the tradition. The core named elements introduced here are digital patterns, the bebop scale, the bebop lick, and enclosures—foundational terminology that organizes the subsequent instructional chapters. The chapter's theoretical framework rests on the claim that a certain grouping of these ideas and approaches, when consistently applied, gives an identity to a style, making jazz language learnable and reproducible rather than ineffable or purely intuitive.

# Chapter 2 — DIGITAL PATTERNS AND SCALAR PATTERNS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces **change-running** (chord-arpeggiating), a foundational improvisation device with five musical functions: tuning the improviser's ear to chord structure, learning chord progressions, providing pickup notes, clarifying harmony to the audience, and reinforcing form for the ensemble. The chapter establishes the core pedagogical method of **digital analysis**—thinking in scale degrees (1-3-5-1 rather than note names) to achieve key-universality, and mandates practicing all exercises in 12 keys with accompaniment tracks sequenced to mirror real-tune harmonic motion (circle-of-fifths and chromatic progressions). Change-running phrases follow three structural freedoms: they need not begin on the root, may omit chord members, and move in any direction (ascending, descending, or both). The chapter prescribes a three-stage practice progression (alone, with exercise track, with play-along tune) and demands complete arpeggiation regimens across four transposition sequences and five extension levels. Critically, students must internalize patterns through mental and aural skill rather than notation, and should supplement structured practice with transcription study—learning how master improvisers deploy these devices by ear alone.

# Chapter 3 — 7-3 RESOLUTION

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 introduces **digital patterns** (David Baker's term) — 4-8 note cells structured by scale-degree number (e.g. 1-2-3-5 over C major gives C-D-E-G) — and **scalar patterns** (longer, scale-based, often "non-terminal" or "perpetual motion" runs). A master inventory of common 4- and 8-note cells is given, with generative rules for retrograde, fifth-rooting, and chord-quality alteration (1-3-5-3 to 1-b3-5-b3 for minor). The chapter establishes the **rhythmic-level rule**: a cell occupies one steady rhythmic level (most often eighth notes), giving a direct duration mapping — a 4-note cell covers two beats, an 8-note cell covers four. Coker draws the diagnostic distinction that digital patterns favor fundamental chord tones while scalar patterns treat all scale degrees as equal. The chapter's quantitative spine is a Coltrane "Giant Steps" analysis showing digital patterns at roughly 35–40% density across the solo, with the remaining 60–65% largely change-running or pattern-adjacent material — load-bearing empirical evidence that pattern study is non-optional, and that even Coltrane practiced these patterns deliberately before applying them to tunes.

# Chapter 4 — 3-b9

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces the **7-3 resolution**, in which the b7 of one chord resolves down a half-step to the 3 of the next chord, most commonly across a II-7 to V7 (and sometimes V7 to I) motion. Coker grounds the technique by contrasting it with Bach's 4-3 suspension and clarifying that the 7-3 is a voice-leading and melodic device, not a harmonic suspension. The chapter's core efficiency argument is that two notes can imply two chords (one note per chord), so the device dramatically compresses change-running into minimal melodic material. Coker shows the device working without bass accompaniment because the listener's ear-memory supplies the implied roots, and he cites Monk's "Round Midnight" (six 7-3 resolutions in the first eight bars) and Gerry Mulligan's pianoless quartet ("Line For Lyons") as canonical compositional and performance applications. The chapter ends with the standard practice directive: master the illustrated examples in all keys and apply them through exercise tracks and tune play-alongs.

# Chapter 5 — BEBOP SCALE

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 deepens 7-3 work and introduces the **3-b9** device — melodic motion from the 3rd to the flatted 9th of a dominant seventh chord, applied bidirectionally and either by leap or by scalar fill. Coker observes that 7-3 resolutions on minor seventh chords often pull in the 3rd because the 3rd and 7th are the chord-defining tones, then teaches the pivotal insight that elements chain via shared pivot notes — the 3 that ends a 7-3 resolution can launch a 3-b9, producing a compound gesture larger than either device alone. The chapter quantifies usage with Parker (15 on "Confirmation"), Rollins (31 on "Eternal Triangle"), and Stitt (16 on the same), establishing 7-3 resolution as a high-frequency structural device rather than ornament. Coker prescribes a graduated self-testing regimen (all keys, then reduced accompaniment, then unaccompanied) so that 7-3 resolutions become the player's tool for hearing a progression solo. The chapter closes with the author's broader pedagogical principle — anchor new vocabulary to already-known melodies — and a directive ranking composed heads above transcribed solos as the most efficient illustrative source for 3-b9 study.

# Chapter 6 — BEBOP LICK

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 introduces the **bebop scale** (David Baker's term and discovery) — a major, dorian, or mixolydian scale with one specific chromatic note added, yielding an eight-note scale. The chapter explains the historical-rhythmic rationale: an eight-note scale fits 4/4 time exactly (four beats of eighth notes) and lands chord tones on downbeats, unlike the seven-note scale. Coker specifies the precise location of the added chromatic note for each variant — between 5 and 6 in major, between 3 and 4 in dorian, and between 7 and 8 in mixolydian — and notes two surprising empirical facts: the chromatic note is ~99% consistent across transcribed solos, and in the dorian and mixolydian forms it is a perfect anachronism (major 3rd against minor 7 chord, major 7 against dominant 7) that contradicts the underlying chord quality. The chapter also covers practice context (cycle of fifths, II-V chains) for the 3-b9 device introduced previously, plus a substitution rule (Track AA uses half-diminished for minor seventh, altered for unaltered dominants) and a worked register-change decision for Exercise 138.

# Chapter 7 — HARMONIC GENERALIZATION

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 elaborates the **bebop scale** with three recognition and pedagogy rules. First, intensified bebop-scale use historically begins in the Bebop Era (1940-1950) but the device predates that period — Coker cites a 1927 Armstrong phrase on "Hotter Than That" — so students should hear it across style periods. Second, a player need not deploy the entire scale to be "using" it: a 3-4 note fragment containing the designated chromatic note already implies bebop-scale use, which dramatically expands recognition and application. Third, the chapter quantifies saturation: Coltrane plays the bebop-scale pattern 21 times in an 8-chorus solo on a 16-bar tune, showing the scale operating as a structural device, not an ornament. Coker dispenses with the customary written illustrations and routes the student directly to recorded examples and to Baker's HOW TO PLAY BEBOP, then specifies cycle-of-fifths transposition practice (Track N) and a critical track-alignment warning so the student does not start exercises in the wrong key against the backing tracks.

# Chapter 8 — ENCLOSURE

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 introduces the **bebop lick** — a specific melodic phrase, closely related to the bebop scale and using a portion of that scale, that typically occurs on dominant seventh and minor seventh chords. Coker credits David Baker with the discovery, naming, and codification of this device through analysis of Bebop-Era and later solos, framing the lick as an emergent (not composed) idiom rather than a deliberately taught construct. The pedagogical sequence is explicit: learn the lick in isolated form first against tracks with a preponderance of minor seventh and dominant seventh chords, then learn to weave it into longer melodic lines preceded and followed by non-bebop-lick substance. Coker extends the lick's application beyond its basic harmonic context by showing it can be used to imply tri-tone substitutions or altered dominants, a forward-pointing concept the book treats in detail later. The chapter calibrates the student's expectations: the lick has low acquisition cost due to its brevity and structural simplicity, and high stylistic payoff — a strong implicit argument for prioritizing it in practice.

# Chapter 9 — SEQUENCE

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 introduces **harmonic generalization** — using a single scale to accommodate two or more chords of a progression — and ranks the strategies from safest to most advanced. The simplest case is the tonic major scale over II-V-I in major (the scales of D dorian and G mixolydian are both C major reindexed), producing no "wrong" notes but possibly weak chord-tone emphasis. Pentatonic generalization is even safer: a single C pentatonic covers ten common chords (C major, F major, Bb maj+4, A-7, D-7, G-7, C7, G7sus, D7sus, Gb7 alt) with no collision tones at all. The blues scale generalizes across blues progressions and, after Parker's example, across the last four measures of "Rhythm Changes" A sections — even with 4-8 underlying chords. The chapter then handles collision-tone cases: harmonic minor over a minor II-V-I works because such progressions are brief, the aggregate effect is minor, and players instinctively avoid the collision tones; an analogous diminished-scale generalization covers just the minor II and V. Coker reframes the device as a deliberate compositional option (not a cop-out), warns that play-alongs play against the grain (a live rhythm section would adapt), and anchors the rules to concrete tunes (Corea's "500 Miles High," Shorter's "Speak No Evil").

# Chapter 10 — CESH

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 introduces **enclosure** — a melodic device that approaches an object note from both the upper leading tone (a half-step above) and the lower leading tone (a half-step below), formalized as the three-note ordering UPPER-LOWER-OBJECT. Coker carefully distinguishes canonical enclosures from near-enclosure approximations that analysts will frequently encounter in transcribed solos, and warns that real enclosures are routinely decorated or embellished, extending past three notes. The chapter places enclosure into the book's broader claim about element combination: jazz-language devices are quickly successive, overlapping, and interlocking, illustrated by an example combining six elements in a two-measure phrase. Quantitative usage data (Rollins 5x in "Airegin," Clifford Brown 35 in four choruses of "Confirmation," Clark Terry 17 in seven blues choruses) sets the normative density target, and Baker's HOW TO PLAY BEBOP is again named as the authoritative supplementary source. The chapter pivots into **sequence** — a melodic fragment immediately followed by one or more variations — and elevates sequence above all other elements as the syntactic layer of jazz, defining it as the level at which the language becomes communicative "sentences" rather than "letters and words." Sequences may be melodic, rhythmic, harmonic, or interval-based, and may be transparent or heavily disguised while still functioning communicatively.

# Chapter 11 — QUOTES

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 completes **sequence** practice and introduces **CESH** (Contrapuntal Elaboration of Static Harmony). For sequences, Coker states the non-negotiable prerequisite — the ability to play any motive in any key with any chord-type — and prescribes a methodical regimen: play tune melodies in all 12 keys, learn to slightly alter motives to fit different chord-types, and practice a single motive throughout complete progressions while varying rhythms and adding or deleting notes. A taxonomy of sequence transformations names three modes (transposition, common-tone stasis under different chords, rhythmic alteration), with Parker exemplifying anchored-pitch sequences with metric displacement and J.J. Johnson showing that even a two-note cell can sequence. The CESH section defines the device etymologically (static harmony = one chord remains; contrapuntal elaboration = one chord member is in motion), enumerates its four varieties (minor and major, each with either root or fifth in motion), and identifies minor-with-root-in-motion as the most common and the focus for study. Coker warns students that CESH appears in incomplete or heavily embellished forms in real solos and pivots the pedagogy by anchoring CESH to familiar standard-repertoire melodies. The chapter closes with a directed listening canon for sequence-rich players (J.J. Johnson, Joe Henderson, Kenny Dorham, Horace Silver, Randy Brecker, Wes Montgomery, Bill Evans).

# Chapter 13 — "GONE BUT NOT FORGOTTEN" LICK

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 closes CESH and introduces **quotes**. Coker frames CESH as a harmonic element with no canonical melodic realization — students learn shared melodic tendencies from solo examples but must also develop their own from the included starter list of CESH patterns to practice in all keys. Quotes are defined as phrases taken from a known tune, deployed when the harmonic setting (chord or progression cell) of the improvised tune matches that of the source. Coker distinguishes two cognitive types: the deliberate humorous interjection (usually appreciated because the listener knows the tune) and the accidental/emergent quote that arises when an improviser stumbles onto a familiar opening interval and decides to complete the allusion. Horace Silver exemplifies the extreme case — Silver works quotes not only into his solos but into his compositions — and Coker warns analysts that partial or implied quotes carry irreducible interpretive ambiguity (even interviewing the player rarely recovers intention). The chapter completes its taxonomy by naming two additional quote varieties: quotes from the melody of the tune being played at the moment, and quotes from well-known recorded solos by influential jazz artists.

# Chapter 14 — OTHER CONSIDERATIONS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 makes the chapter's central pedagogical claim about **quotes**: of all the elements taken up in the book, quotes are among the hardest to learn — not because finding harmonic settings or transposing the phrase is difficult, but because the prerequisite is broad tune knowledge. Coker turns this into a professional consequence: contractors hiring young players complain about a deplorable lack of tune repertoire, and such players get hired once but never again. He operationalizes "broad repertoire" by cataloguing the cross-genre reach of bebop masters — Gillespie and Hubbard quoting Bizet, Silver quoting a Chopin Prelude, Parker quoting the standard "Honey," Bill Evans quoting "March of the Grenadiers" — alongside extensive quotation of jazz originals and revered recorded solos. The chapter thus shifts the burden of quote-study from technique to repertoire-acquisition, framing tune knowledge as both an aesthetic and career requirement.

# Chapter 15 — SAMPLE ANALYSES OF TWO TRANSCRIBED SOLOS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Chapter 15 covers the **named-lick** chapters ("Cry Me A River" and "Gone But Not Forgotten") and a cluster of chromatic-substitution devices. Quote competence is framed as a three-trait skill: farcical-yet-poetic humor, quick-witted real-time recognition, and harmonic-setting awareness; spontaneous quotes arise because every possible opening interval is shared with many tunes, and harmonic context governs which quotes fit. The **Cry Me A River** lick — opening melody of the tune — is uniquely valuable because it accommodates five different chord-types without alteration (starting on the 9 of -7, 13 of 7, 4 of major, +9 of altered dominant, and major 7 of #5 major); the **Gone But Not Forgotten** lick works in the same five contexts. Both are practiced in all 12 keys against specified tracks (G, H, I, J, K, L, W, X, BB). The chapter then defines load-bearing harmonic devices: **linear chromaticism** as metric-fill (echoing the bebop-scale rationale), **tri-tone substitution** (replacing a dominant 7 with another dominant 7 a tritone away), the **altered dominant** (+5, b9, +9, +11) as structurally near-identical to the tri-tone substitute, the **back door progression** (IV-7 to bVII7 substituting for V7), and **#II°7** as a V7 substitute. Coker explains why these substitutions sound consonant against the original V7: the listener's ear hears the substitute's familiar precedent rather than the conflict with the V7 chord.

# Chapter 16 — APPENDIX A - CHORD PROGRESSIONS FOR ALL PLAY-ALONG TRACKS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

Chapter 16 covers **other considerations** — bar-line shifts, side-slipping, and errors — plus the framing for solo analysis. **Bar-line shifts** are reframed as legitimate phenomena (not novice errors); they arise from two accidental causes (harmonic generalization across a II-V or a momentary pause leading to a "better late than never" entry) or as deliberate tension devices, exemplified by Cannonball Adderley's intentional half-step displacement of the bridge of "So What." The analyst's procedural rule is to inspect chords both before and after a suspected error before judging. **Side-slipping** and **outside playing** are defined as the same deliberate out-of-key tension device, and Coker provides the twelve-minus-seven arithmetic: with seven scale tones accommodating most chords, five "wrong" notes remain, to be used in convincing groups or sparingly with smooth delivery. **Errors** are defined residually — what cannot be reconciled to any named chromatic device — with two observable cues: inexplicable space after a wrong note (the player's natural balk) and incorrect harmonic placement of a known phrase like the Cry Me A River lick. The chapter ends with the book's framing claim that the catalogued elements are connective tissue — what remains in a master's solo after tagging all elements is often the most original material and must be studied with equal care. A well-constructed solo should exhibit a significant subset of catalogued elements, and individual style emerges from how each player emphasizes, omits, or transforms them.

# Chapter 17 — APPENDIX B - APPLYING ELEMENTS OF THE JAZZ LANGUAGE TO TUNE PROGRESSIONS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

Chapter 17 (Appendix B prose) establishes the book's master practice methodology and the conventions for the annotated tune progressions. Coker's foundational rule is that elements must be ingrained into the mind, ears, and hands — reading the book is insufficient and the memory loses what the hands have not learned. He defines a four-stage assimilation process: (1) mental understanding through definitions and illustrations, (2) motivational reinforcement by hearing the element in master solos, (3) hands-on practice in all keys, tempos, and registers, (4) ear-to-harmony association through play-along exercises (Appendix A), culminating in (5) application to real tune progressions (Appendix B). Each of the three included progressions appears in two versions — a first version with only chord symbols and recommended scales for harmonic internalization, then an annotated second version showing element placement — so the student moves from contrivance to spontaneous recognition. The chapter codifies notational conventions: scales may not start on chord roots (the root is the weakest chord tone), enharmonic spellings minimize accidentals, a single scale covers a one-measure II-V (a form of harmonic generalization), and filled-in noteheads flag chords shorter than four beats. A master abbreviation legend (7-3, BBL, HG, CMAR, GBNF, TT/AD, BD, #II°) plus parenthetical-annotation parsing rules (with worked examples) decode the second-version charts. Coker explicitly lists the elements omitted from annotations because their use is location-independent (sequences, change-running, digital patterns, scalar patterns, bebop scale, enclosures, quotes, linear chromaticism, bar-line shifts, side-slipping), warning that absence from charts does not mean lesser importance. Footnotes provide a Back-Door substitution melodic rule (CMAR or GBNF starting on the 9 of the substitute, BBL on the 4) and explicitly authorize voice-leading-justified GBNF use beyond its canonical context.

## Change-Running Definition

> Change is a synonym for chord. Running is a synonym for arpeggiating. Hence change-

> expression, change-running is somewhat superfluous when it occurs in an improvised
> solo, since the chord is already being sounded and/or implied by members of the rhythm
> section (piano, guitar, and bass).

## Functions of Change-Running

> It has at least several possible
> functions: (1) as a phrase which helps to place the ear of the improviser into the exact
> structure and sound of a chord, which sometimes insures the effectiveness and accuracy
> of a more melodic phrase to follow; (2) as a means to learn, during practice, the sound of
> each chord in a sequence or progression of chords; (3) as pick-up notes into a melodic
> phrase; (4) as a means to make the sound of a chord clear to an audience; and (5) as a
> means of communication or reinforcement for other members of the group, in the event
> that one or more members lose their place in the progression or the form of the tune.

**Significance (p. 8).** Enumerates five musical purposes of change-running.

> members, and its direction can be ascending or descending or both.

## Bebop Scale Definition

> A common scale (major, dorian, or mixolydian, usually) that has one specific
> chromatic tone (non-harmonic) added, causing the scale to have eight notes, rather than
> the usual seven. The discovery of the scale’s existence in improvised solos, as well as the
> designated term for the scale, are credited to David Baker, who has written much about
> the scale and its use in HOW TO PLAY BEBOP.

**Significance (p. 33).** Foundational definition of bebop scale.

## Why Eight Notes: Rhythmic Rationale

> The development of the scale,
> historically, came about as a result of the need to use an eight-note scale, instead ofa
> seven-note scale, in order to fit a 4/4 time signature (which can be consumed by 8 eighth-
> notes). In other words, the bebop scale will equal four beats, instead of the three and
> one-half beats that result from using a seven-note scale.

**Significance (p. 33).** Rhythmic rationale for the bebop scale's eight-note construction.

## Added Chromatic Note by Scale Type

> The added chromatic note in the major scale is the half-step between the fifth and sixth
> degrees of the scale. In the dorian scale, the added note is the half-step between the
> third and fourth degree, and in the mixolydian scale it’s the half-step between the seventh
> degree and the octave of the first degree.

**Significance (p. 33).** Precise location of chromatic insertion for each variant.

## Chromatic Note Consistency & Anachronism

> Two surprising facts emerge when studying the bebop scale: (1) the added chromatic
> note, with respect its specificity, is 9914% consistent with what we find in analyzing
> transcribed solos; and (2) the added note, in the case of the dorian and mixolydian, is the
> perfect anachronism to the chord quality with which it is used; that is, a major third
> against a minor seventh chord or a major seventh against a dominant seventh are
> precisely the notes we generally consider to be contrary to the chord quality.

**Significance (p. 33).** Two empirical claims justifying bebop scale as a distinct teachable concept.

**Significance (p. 31).** Prescriptive harmonic contexts for 3-b9 practice.

**Significance (p. 32).** Substitution rule for half-diminished and altered dominant variants.

## Three traits for using quotes

> players who use quotes well generally have
> three important traits/skills in their personalities and performance abilities:
>
> (1) a farcical, yet poetic, sense of humor;
>
> (2) quick-witted reactions to events of the moment, along with the grace and
> presence of mind to utilize those reactions in an imaginative way; and
>
> (3) recognition of the harmonic setting that will accommodate the quote
> being used.

**Significance (p. 73).** Three traits required to quote effectively.

## Recognizing quote allusions in improvisation

> Every possible interval has been used in the opening two notes of at least one familiar
> tune, if not many. Hence the first two notes of an improvised phrase already shares that
> opening interval with one or more known tunes. Even longer series of notes (3 or more)
> are often shared by several known tunes. So when an improviser happens upon 2 or
> more notes which occur in some other song(s), and he/she is familiar with the song,
> quick-wittedness will enable the improviser to recognize the allusion and, perhaps, decide
> to complete the allusion by executing the remainder of the phrase.

**Significance (p. 73).** Mechanism of spontaneous quoting via opening-interval recognition.

## Harmonic setting and quote placement

> Often the initial “allusion”, whether planned or accidental, is made more obvious and
> recognizable by playing it over the same harmonic setting in which the idea originally
> occurred. For example, the first phrase of the standard tune, “Laura”, utilized the
> harmonic setting of II-V-I (in major). Therefore, any time a II-V-I progression occurs (and
> there are many), the opening phrase of “Laura” becomes one of many possibilities for a
> quote when playing another tune which uses the same progression.

**Significance (p. 73).** Harmonic context governs which quotes are appropriate.

## "Cry Me A River" lick definition

> The “Cry Me A River” Lick is a specific melodic fragment, named after the tune from
>
> which it comes. The lick is the opening melodic statement of the tune, “Cry Me A River”
> (see illustration below).

**Significance (p. 73).** Definition of named stock lick.

> There are two reasons: (1) the frequency of its use (and by all players); and (2) its
>
> extraordinary versatility, capable of accommodating five different chord-types without
> being altered!

**Significance (p. 73).** Why the lick is high-value: cross-harmonic applicability.

## "Cry Me A River" lick chord-tone starting points

> Lick starts on the:
> ninth of the -7 chord
> thirteenth of the 7 chord
> fourth of the 2 chord
> augmented ninth of the 7 (+5, +9) chord
> major seventh of the 4 (+5) chord

**Significance (p. 73).** Enumerates same lick across five chord qualities.

> The approach is simple. Practice the lick in all twelve keys. Then study the possible
> harmonic applications as shown in #293. Practice the lick with Tracks G, H, I, J, K, L,
> W}5, X15, and BB, and tune play-alongs which have those harmonic settings.

**Significance (p. 73).** Practice prescription for stock lick.

>
> below).

**Significance (p. 78).** Second named stock lick parallel to Cry Me A River.

>
> River” lick, without being altered.

**Significance (p. 78).** Same harmonic versatility rule applies.

> notes. Similar to the principle of the bebop scale, chromatic notes are often the result of
> a metric problem that results in adding one or more notes to cause the phrase to agree
> with the number of beats in a measure.

**Significance (p. 81).** Defines chromaticism as a metric structural necessity.

## Tri-tone substitution definition

> Tri-tone substitution is the substituting, especially of dominant seventh chords, with a
> chord of the-same type whose root is a tri-tone (diminished fifth or augmented fourth
> interval, or simply three whole-steps)away from the given chord, as in substituting an
> F #7 for a C7.

**Significance (p. 82).** Definition of tri-tone substitution.

**Significance (p. 82).** Altered dominant chord-tone inventory + tri-tone equivalence.

**Significance (p. 82).** Back door progression as V7 substitute.

**Significance (p. 82).** Second common V7 substitution device.

## Bar-Line Shifts: definition

> Bar-line shifts occur when an improviser, by virtue of his/her note choices, arrives at a
> given chord late, sometimes even a whole measure late, or earlier than the given
> placement. Though not intentional, necessarily, they are not errors, either, as they might
> be in the case of the novice who momentarily loses his/her place in the progression.

**Significance (p. 85).** Defines bar-line shifts as legitimate, not errors.

## Bar-Line Shifts: two causes

> Most of the tlme such an event is attributable to one of two causes:(1) the player is using
> harmonic generalization, as in the case of playing a II#7 to V7 (+5, +9) progression as
> only a V7 (+4, +9); or (2) the player wanted to play the previous chord (though it has
> already transpired), but was either pausing momentarily (as in taking a breath), and
> decides to adopt the “better late than never” attitude.

**Significance (p. 85).** Two mechanical causes of accidental bar-line shifts.

## Deliberate Bar-Line Shifts: Adderley example

> At other times a bar-line shift may
> be very intentional, as in Cannonball Adderley’s solo on “So What”, in which he
> deliberately dnters and exits the bridge early, causing considerable tension, since the
> chord of the (A section (D-) is one-half step lower than the chord of the bridge (Eb-)

**Significance (p. 85).** Canonical example of intentional bar-line shift as tension device.

## Analyst rule: check surrounding chords

> person who analyzes should always look at the chords both before and
> after a point Where an error is suspected, before jumping to a wrong conclusion (or
> simply learn fo understand and appreciate deliberate bar-line shifts).

**Significance (p. 85).** Procedural analyst rule for context-checking.

## Side-Slipping / Outside Playing: definition

> Having esgentially the same meaning, these terms refer to events in a solo where the
> improviser ig deliberately playing ‘out-of-the-key’ for the sake of creating tension

**Significance (p. 85).** Definitional baseline for outside playing.

## Outside Playing: wrong-notes arithmetic

> Side-slipping and outside playing derive their note content simply by
> focusing on {he only ‘wrong’ notes of a chord. Most chords are accommodated by a
> seven-note stale. Since there are twelve notes in the chromatic scale, simple subtraction
> reveals that there are five ‘wrong’ notes possible with the average chord. The improviser
> who wants tp side-slip or play outside needs to know what notes are ‘wrong’ with any
> given chord, using those notes in convincing groups of ‘wrong’ notes, as well as learning
> to use them kingly in a mildly dissonant, but smooth manner.

**Significance (p. 85).** Twelve-minus-seven arithmetic and execution requirements.

## Errors: residual definition

> We can b@ reasonably certain that an error has occurred if the note(s) cannot be
> reconciled t) any of the foregoing types of chromatic devices.

**Significance (p. 85).** Residual criterion for errors after ruling out chromatic devices.

## Errors: pedagogical value

> ven errors teach us that some things work and others don’t, and disrespect
> is not the ndtural conclusion of learning that al/ of us are imperfect and capable of
> making errars. Nor do we want to underestimate the difficulty of the craft. If a master
> like John Coltrane can make an error, then the rest of us are on notice to be doubly
> careful about presuming our preparedness to be perfect! Furthermore, no great player
> wants us tofimitate, unknowingly, his imperfections. He’d rather we focused on his best
> efforts. If we don’t know an error when confronted with one, then how can we ignore
> that event and concentrate on the finest points?

**Significance (p. 85).** Rationale for studying errors to avoid imitating imperfections.

## Error cue: space after wrong note

> Single-note errors can often be spotted by the appearance of inexplicable space after a
> wrong note, as it is a natural reaction of any improviser to balk after unintentional errors.

**Significance (p. 85).** Observable cue: post-note silence signals single-note error.

## Known-phrase misapplication as error

> The other type of error about which we can be reasonably certain is the instance in
> which a known, ‘right’ phrase is applied incorrectly. For example, the “Cry Me A River”
> lick is a familiar, well-established phrase, capable of sounding correctly with at least five
> different chords. But if the application analyzed doesn’t agree with any of the five
> applications, and the harmonic setting causes several of the notes of the lick to be in
> conflict with the chord, it is reasonable to assume that the lick was placed in the wrong

**Significance (p. 85).** Pattern-based test for errors and analyst directive.

## Elements as connective tissue

> the elements of the jazz language
> taken up here, primarily represent the connective tissue of the language. They can and
> are handled creatively much of the time, but the most creative, original, and inspired
> portions of a given solo are often what remains after you’ve tagged all the elements.
>
> Therefore, “what remains” must be carefully studied, perhaps more than the elements of

**Significance (p. 85).** Reframes catalogue as infrastructure — creativity lies beyond it.

## Solo analysis methodology

> If the elements put forth in this book are truly the major elements of the jazz language,
> then the analysis of a solo will reveal that many of those elements will appear in the solo.

**Significance (p. 85).** Falsifiable prediction validating the book's framework.

## Individual style and element emphasis

> The ardent student should be advised that certain players emphasize certain of the
> elements in their solos, each player being anywhere from slightly to dramatically different

> Therefore, a student who wishes to understand an individual improviser’s style should
> learn to recognize those differences and emphases as part of his/her study.

**Significance (p. 85).** Element frequency/selection as style signature.

# Chapter 17 — APPENDIX B - APPLYING ELEMENTS OF THE JAZZ LANGUAGE TO TUNE PROGRESSIONS

_Pages 116-141._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. ## Practice imperative — beyond reading

> If the elements of the jazz language, as presented in this book, are to become useful to
> the developing improviser, they must be ingrained into the mind, ears, and hands. Merely
> reading the book, even with the deepest understanding, will not reap the necessary
> benefits of this sort of study. Indeed it is even unlikely that they will remain, for very long,
> in the memory. Improvisation is a performer’s art. Hence it becomes imperative for the
> serious student of improvisation that the first step, that of mentally comprehending each
> element, be followed by diligent study and practice, in order that it become assimilated
> by, and subsequently useful to, the developing improviser. One can mentally decide that
> an element of the jazz language ought to become part of his/her improvisation, even plan
> to use it soon, but the mind and ears still need to establish the habit of considering the
> element’s use, and the hands need to feel comfortable with the execution of the element
> before it will materialize in the ‘heat of battle’ as part of an improvised solo.

**Significance (p. 116).** Reading is insufficient; physical/aural habit required.

## Four-stage element assimilation

> The absorption of an element happens in stages. First it must be mentally understood
> through definitions and illustrations. Then motivational fires need to be fueled by
> observing (and hopefully hearing) the element as it has been used in seemingly endless
> variations by masters of the craft. In the next stage, the ‘hands on’ approach begins, with
> the student practicing the execution of the element, including its variations, at different
> tempos, and in all keys and registers of the instrument. Then a strong association needs
> to be established and developed in the ear, connecting the sound of the element with the
> sound of its accompanying harmony. This is accomplished through playing the element
> with exercise play-alongs, like those found in Appendix A. Finally, and now we come to
> the function of Appendix B, each element should be applied to ‘real’ chord progressions
> of tunes. In this last stage of assimilation the student learns to discern locations within
> the progression which accommodate the playing of certain elements (unlike exercise
> play-alongs, tunes usually have a generous mixture of various chord-types, durations,
> etc.; therefore, one has to search out opportunities within the progression where,
> potentially anyway, a certain element becomes possible).

**Significance (p. 116).** Four sequential stages of element absorption.

## Two-version progression pedagogy

> Each of the 3 progressions is presented in two versions. In the first version only the
> chord symbols and recommended scales are given. The student should practice
> improvising with this first version for awhile without considering the use of elements, until
> the chords and scales are familiar and comfortable to the player. The second written
> version (to be used with the same cassette track as the first version) gives the chord
> symbols and indicates where certain elements can be used, so the student can practice
> working them into an improvisation. Eventually, of course, the process will become more
> natural, rather than a contrivance, and the improviser learns to recognize potential
> locations for various elements in any progression, whether or not he/she chooses to
> execute the thought.

**Significance (p. 116).** Two-pass method: internalize harmony then layer elements.

## Avoiding over-use of root

> some of the scales and their
> spellings may /Jook strange or wrong to the reader, but they are indeed wholly accurate.
> Sometimes the given scales do not begin on the roots of the chords, but this author
> believes that when all scales are thought of as beginning on the roots of the chords, the
> player is more subject to over-using the root, which is a questionable practice, since the
> root is generally the weakest note of a chord, sounding simplistic and redundant.

**Significance (p. 116).** Rule: root is weakest chord tone; non-root scale orientation justified.

## Enharmonic scale spelling rationale

> other times the given scale may be written enharmonically (i.e., Db instead of C4),
> sometimes confusing the reader at first, but the rationale for writing, say, a Db lydian
> augmented scale on an altered A7 chord instead of a C# lydian augmented scale, is that
> the one selected has fewer accidentals, though it may appear to be a ‘flat’ scale ona
> ‘sharp’ chord.

**Significance (p. 117).** Notational rule: fewest accidentals wins.

## II-V harmonic generalization — one scale

> Whenever a II-V progression occurs in one measure (2 beats for each chord), only one
> scale is given for the whole measure, since both chords are accommodated by the same
> pitches. For example, the notes of a Bb dorian scale (for a Bb-7) are identical to the notes
> of the Eb mixolydian or dominant scale (for an Eb7). The reader may wish to regard this
> practice as a form of harmonic generalization (see Chapter 7).

**Significance (p. 117).** II-V single-scale rule tied to harmonic generalization.

## Filled noteheads — shorter chord duration

> Whenever two chords occur in the same measure (2 beats each) that do not use the
> same scale content, the note heads are filled-in (black) to bring the player’s attention to
> the fact that the chords are of shorter duration than 4 beats each.

**Significance (p. 117).** Notational convention for reading chord durations.

## Tri-tone substitution built-in examples

> “Urbane Blues” has 2 ‘built-in’ examples of tri-tone substitution (see Chapter 12), one
> occurring in the fourth measure, the other in the 10th measure.

**Significance (p. 117).** Specific measure-level tri-tone substitution examples in the tune.

## Element abbreviation legend

> 7-3 = 7-3 resolution
>
> BBL = Bebop Lick
>
> HG = Harmonic Generalization (area will also be bracketed)
> CMAR = Cry Me A River lick
>
> GBNF = Gone But Not Forgotten lick
>
> TT/AD = Tri-Tone substitution/Altered Dominant
>
> BD = Back Door substitution
>
> #II° = #II° substitution
>
> Of course 3-b9 and CESH are already abbreviated terms, needing no explanation.

**Significance (p. 117).** Master legend for decoding annotated progressions.

## Annotation parenthesis conventions

> When a letter, chord symbol, scale abbreviation (i.e. hm for harmonic minor), or key
> area (i.e. Ab major) appears in parenthesis after the element abbreviation, it is to help the
> student to find starting notes, substitute chords or scales, etc. BBL(C), for example,
> would indicate that a bebop lick may be played, starting on the note C. 3-b9 (A-Gb)
> would help the student to know that a 3-b9 is possible, based on the notes A (3rd) and
> Gb (69), if the given chord was F7. HG (F hm) would indicate an opportunity to use
> harmonic generalization, using an F harmonic minor scale. BD(F-7) would tell the player
> that he/she could use a back door substitution, starting on an F-7.

**Significance (p. 117).** Parenthetical annotation parsing rules with worked examples.

## Universal-location elements — not annotated

> The following elements do not appear in the second versions of the progressions

> sequences

>

**Significance (p. 117).** Lists location-independent elements absent from charts but equally important.

## Back Door substitution melodic rule

> ** Whenever a BD is chosen, CMAR or GBNF may be played, starting on 9th of BD substitute, or a BBL on the 4th.

**Significance (p. 119).** Melodic rule for what to play over a Back Door substitution.
